title: Domain Compromise Kill-Chain (Windows capstone)
briefing: Capstone. In one window $TARGET saw the whole Windows kill chain off stolen credentials: a Pass-the-Hash NTLM network logon for $USER1 from an unfamiliar workstation, then a Pass-the-Ticket explicit-credential logon, then a DCSync replication request (4662) pulling directory secrets, then a masqueraded scheduled task for persistence, and finally Windows Defender's real-time protection switched off to cover the payload. Reconstruct the chain, order it, judge the blast radius, and build a containment plan that assumes domain-wide compromise.
dashboard_hint: agent.name: $TARGET, sort by @timestamp. Expect: 100135 PtH NTLM type3 logon -> 100136 PtT type9 explicit-cred logon -> 100138 DCSync 4662 (the Replicating Directory Changes GUID 1131f6aa..., requester is a USER not a DC) -> 100139 4698 scheduled task (encoded powershell) -> 100170 Defender 5001 disabled. The DCSync is the pivot: once krbtgt can be pulled, assume the whole domain is owned.

ground_truth.attack_type: Full Windows domain compromise (stolen creds to DCSync to persistence)

options: Full domain kill-chain: credential reuse -> lateral logon -> DCSync -> persistence -> defense evasion
questions.answer: Full domain kill-chain: credential reuse -> lateral logon -> DCSync -> persistence -> defense evasion
questions.explain: PtH + PtT logons, a DCSync secret pull, a persistence task, and Defender being disabled together form a complete domain-compromise chain from stolen creds to entrenchment.
All events land on $TARGET, though the DCSync means the impact is domain-wide, not just this host.
All five injected stages are present; no encryption/ransomware stage occurred.

questions.prompt: Which event is the most severe and why?
options: The DCSync — it can pull krbtgt and hand over the whole domain
The first NTLM logon — it started it
The scheduled task — persistence is worst
questions.answer: The DCSync — it can pull krbtgt and hand over the whole domain
questions.explain: DCSync (T1003.006) lets the attacker extract the krbtgt hash and forge Golden Tickets, converting host access into persistent domain-wide control.

questions.prompt: You reset $USER1 and delete the scheduled task. Is the domain safe?
options: No — if krbtgt was pulled, forged tickets survive any single account reset
Yes — resetting the abused account ends it
Yes — deleting the task is enough
No — but only the Defender change matters
questions.answer: No — if krbtgt was pulled, forged tickets survive any single account reset
questions.explain: Golden Tickets minted from a stolen krbtgt hash bypass account resets; only a double krbtgt rotation invalidates them.
Each stage maps to its technique, all built on valid/stolen accounts (T1078) — every option applies.

options: Isolate $TARGET and preserve a forensic image
Rotate krbtgt twice and force a domain-wide privileged-credential reset
Reset $USER1 and strip any unneeded replication rights
Re-enable Defender + tamper protection and delete the rogue task
Hunt for Golden Ticket use and the same TTPs on other hosts
Close the alert — replication is normal
answer: Isolate $TARGET and preserve a forensic image
Rotate krbtgt twice and force a domain-wide privileged-credential reset
Reset $USER1 and strip any unneeded replication rights
Re-enable Defender + tamper protection and delete the rogue task
Hunt for Golden Ticket use and the same TTPs on other hosts
questions.explain: Assume domain compromise: isolate and image, double-rotate krbtgt and mass-reset privileged creds, remove the abused rights and persistence, restore AV, and hunt for forged tickets and lateral spread.
